Standardized performance across batting and pitching. Positive = above league average, negative = below.

X = total batting Z, Y = total pitching Z, bubble size = combined total. Top right = best overall.

Site Guide
How each page works, key calculations, and what's new.
Site Flow — how everything connects

Six layers, top to bottom. Each builds on the one above it. Raw data comes in at the top; daily decisions fall out at the bottom. Skim the diagram first, then the section notes underneath fill in the "why."

1. Raw data. Four external feeds. Fantrax is the system of record — standings, weekly stats, rosters, transaction history. MLB Stats API provides today's probable pitchers and the league schedule. FantasyPros contributes consensus rest-of-season projections used for forward-looking SGP. FanGraphs supplies fWAR (the player-rating gold standard) and Statcast (xBA / xSLG / xwOBA used for buy-low signals). All four are pulled by a GitHub Action and stored as JSON files the site reads directly.
2. League analytics. Before the math layer can weight anything correctly, you need to know what's actually winning games this season. Z-Scores show how each team performs in each category relative to league mean — the raw shape of the standings. JOE Factor measures schedule luck: how much each team's record reflects their underlying performance vs. who they happened to face when. These two signals tell the math layer below which categories matter and where the real talent (vs. luck) sits.
3. Math & models. The transformation engine. SGP converts raw category stats into "standings gain points" — a single comparable unit. Correlations identify which cats actually drive winning. Quartile bucketing turns continuous values into Tough/Above-avg/Avg/Easy matchup tiers. The ROS + current blend weights forward-looking projections 50/50 with current-season production. Position scarcity bumps up players at thin positions (a top SS is worth more than a top OF). Replacement level sets the baseline of what's freely available on waivers, so SGP is measured above-replacement rather than absolute. JOE factor normalizes for schedule luck.
4. Player metrics. Five derived numbers per player, each measuring a different facet. fWAR% is a percentile of true talent within the player's position group. Lg Rating% is FantasyPros consensus consensus. Pos rating is rank within the position. ROS SGP is forward-looking value, Curr SGP is recent-form value. All five converge into Today-score: the per-game expected SGP gain blended 50/50 ROS+current and multiplied by today's matchup tier. This is the number the daily decision tools rank by.
5. Team views. Roster-level aggregations. Team Roster is the full player list with ratings. Team Archetype classifies your team's shape (Heavyweight, Cinderella, Pretender, etc.) and recommends a strategic posture (Buy / Sell / Hold). Team Need identifies positional gaps (where roster is below league average) and category gaps (which standings cats you most need to add to). The Team Need output specifically becomes weighting input for the decision tools below.
6. Daily decisions. Where it all lands. Lineup Organizer takes today-score, filters out IL players, applies position-eligibility, and ranks your roster into a slot-filled lineup with toss-up flags. Add / Drop ranks waiver candidates by SGP gain weighted by your team's category needs — not just "is this guy good" but "does he add what you specifically need." Trade analyzer highlights buy-low / sell-high opportunities by comparing each player's actual stats to Statcast expected stats — a gap signals likely regression or improvement.
Player metrics — concise definitions

The six numbers that show up most across the site. Each measures something different about a player; together they're what feeds Today-score and the decision tools.

fWAR
What it is: Wins Above Replacement, FanGraphs version. A single number that captures a player's total contribution above a freely-available replacement-level player. Hitters get value for offense + defense + baserunning + position; pitchers for runs prevented.
Why it matters: The closest thing to a unified "how good is this player" metric. Used as the gold-standard input for ratings.
fWAR%
What it is: A player's fWAR expressed as a percentile within their position group. A SS at 95% fWAR% is in the top 5% of shortstops; an OF at 95% is in the top 5% of outfielders.
Why it matters: Normalizes for positional context. A 3.0 fWAR catcher and a 3.0 fWAR outfielder have very different value to your roster; the percentile makes them comparable as "both elite at their spot."
Pos Rating
What it is: A player's rank among everyone eligible at their primary position (e.g. "3rd of 28 rostered SS"). Pure ordinal placement.
Why it matters: Tells you whether a player is genuinely scarce at their slot or just a generic body. Critical for trade math when a top-3 catcher might be worth more in your league than a top-10 outfielder despite similar overall stats.
League Rating
What it is: FantasyPros consensus rating — a weighted aggregate of how the fantasy industry's analysts rank the player. Comes back as a percentile (0–100) within the player pool.
Why it matters: Independent check on fWAR. fWAR measures past production; League Rating folds in scout consensus and forward-looking opinion. When the two diverge, that's information — a player can be elite by fWAR but suspect by consensus, or vice versa.
Curr SGP
What it is: Standings Gain Points based on this season's actual stats. Converts a player's R/HR/RBI/SB/AVG (and W/K/SVH3/ERA/WHIP for pitchers) into a single value reflecting how many standings places they would gain you if added to a league-average team. Scaled by playing time.
Why it matters: Captures recent form and breakouts. A rookie with three great starts is doing real damage right now even if projection systems haven't caught up.
ROS SGP
What it is: Same SGP calculation, but using FantasyPros' rest-of-season projections instead of actual stats. Forward-looking expectation of standings impact.
Why it matters: Stable baseline that regresses fluky hot streaks toward true talent. Prevents over-reacting to a 3-game small sample, but too conservative on its own — which is why Today-score blends it 50/50 with Curr SGP.

How they combine: fWAR%, League Rating, and Pos Rating together describe who a player is. ROS SGP and Curr SGP together (50/50 blend) describe how much value they add per game. Multiplied by a daily matchup tier, that becomes Today-score — the number the Lineup Organizer ranks by.

Welcome to Bluebonnet Baseball Analytics

This site is a full analytics platform for the Bluebonnet Baseball Federation - a 10-team rotisserie league scoring 10 categories over 20 weekly periods. Every page connects to live Fantrax data fetched automatically by a GitHub Action.

WEEKLY WORKFLOW - What to check each week
1Dashboard - See your current matchup, standings position, and top players at a glance.
2Match Predictions - Check win probability vs your current opponent. Which categories are you likely to lose?
3SGP → By Position - Is there a free agent or trade target who upgrades your weakest slot?
4Trade Waiver Wire Targets - Any underperforming hitters or pitchers worth picking up?
5JOE Factor - Are you running lucky or unlucky? Adjust expectations accordingly.
10 Scoring Categories
Hitting: R · HR · RBI · SB · AVG
Pitching: W · K · SVH3 · ERA · WHIP
Pages Overview
Dashboard - Your personal command center. Select your team.
League Leaders - Top individual + team stats this season.
Fantrax Data - Raw Fantrax standings, stats, historic data (2021-2025).
League Stats - Week-by-week stats, H2H matrix, matchup history.
Z-Scores - How each team compares to league average per category.
JOE Factor - Scheduling luck analysis. Are you winning/losing because of schedule?
Match Predictions - Win probabilities for every period matchup.
Correlations - Which stats drive standings? Current + 5-year historical.
fWAR - Fantasy Wins Above Replacement. The core player valuation model.
Rosters - Full rosters with fWAR and Statcast data.
Player Trade & Market - SGP waiver engine, trade analyzer, free agent recs.
Trade Waiver Wire Targets - Statcast buy-low / sell-high signals for hitters + pitchers.
Dashboard overview
There are two dashboards. The Dashboard tab shows your team's live stats, matchup, JOE factor luck, positional strength, top fWAR players, trade suggestions, and waiver targets. The New Dashboard tab shows a roster health view with drop candidates, add targets, and positional strength vs the league. Both update when you change team from the dropdown. Use Refresh All Data at the top of either dashboard to run all data pipelines at once — this ensures every chart and table is populated before you navigate to other pages.
Roster overview — how to read the charts

The Roster Overview card appears on both dashboards. It contains five panels: two donuts, two radars, and a scatter plot.

The two donut charts

The left donut shows fWAR by group — how your total fantasy value is distributed across Infield, Outfield, SP, and RP. A large IF+OF slice means you are a hitting-driven team. A large SP slice means your pitchers are carrying the team.

The right donut shows positional strength by group — the average player rating of your rostered players in each group as a percentile. This reflects depth and quality, not just total output. A balanced donut means even depth across all four groups.

Key insight: compare the two donuts. If fWAR is hitter-heavy but positional strength is balanced, your pitchers have good ratings but low fantasy output — they are underperforming. If fWAR is pitcher-heavy but positional strength shows weak pitching, a few elite arms are masking thin depth behind them.

Example: hitter-heavy fWAR, balanced positional strength
fWAR by group
IF +38, OF +31, SP +5, RP +4
IF +38 OF +31 SP +5 RP +4
Positional strength by group
IF 52, OF 54, SP 49, RP 47
IF 52 OF 54 SP 49 RP 47

The fWAR donut is dominated by IF+OF (88% of value from hitters) while the positional strength donut is nearly equal quarters. Pitchers have decent ratings but very low fantasy output. Expect to compete in hitting categories and struggle in ERA, WHIP, K, W, SVH3.

The radar charts

Each radar has two lines. The solid line (navy for hitting, green for pitching) shows positional strength — your average player rating percentile in each category. The dashed red line shows fWAR% — how your players are actually performing relative to all rostered players at their position.

Larger always means better — including for ERA and WHIP. A high ERA percentile means your ERA is low (good), because the z-score conversion inverts these stats before plotting.

The gap between the two lines is the key signal. When fWAR% is inside positional strength, players are underperforming their ratings. When fWAR% bulges outside, players are overperforming.

Example: underperforming (fWAR% inside)
Pos strength large, fWAR% small inside — pitchers underperforming ratings.
Pos Strength fWAR%

Well-rated pitchers not delivering. Sell high on reputation — regression risk or injury candidates.

Example: overperforming (fWAR% outside)
fWAR% bulging outside pos strength — pitchers overperforming their ratings.
Pos Strength fWAR%

Lower-rated pitchers outperforming expectations. Could be a breakout or luck-driven — cross-check with JOE Factor before trading.

Team archetypes — what to expect from your Z-scores

The donuts and Z-scores tell a story together. Here are the most common team archetypes — use them to diagnose your roster and identify what to do next.

Loading archetype charts...
Other dashboard cards
Current matchup
Shows your live or most recent scoring period matchup. Green/red header = winning/losing. Each category is coloured — green means you are winning that category, red means losing. Win/loss tally shown in the header badge.
Category breakdown vs league avg
Bars show your season-to-date stat total vs the league average. Green = above average. Red = below. ERA and WHIP are inverted — a green bar means your ERA is lower than average (good).
JOE Factor luck
Your normalized season-average JOE score (−1 to +1). Positive = unlucky — you would have more wins with an average schedule. Negative = lucky — your record is flattered by a weak schedule. See the JOE Factor tab for the full breakdown.
Top 5 by fWAR
Your top 5 hitters and pitchers by Fantasy WAR. Requires the fWAR pipeline to have run — use Refresh All Data if empty. Columns: fWAR · fWAR-Pts (estimated fantasy pts contribution) · Z-score · key stats.
Trade suggestions & waiver targets
Top mutual-benefit trade suggestions targeting your weakest positional groups, and best available free agents for those same positions. The number on the right of each trade shows your net rating gain.
Drop candidates (New Dashboard)
Players in the bottom 20% of your roster by fWAR%. Sorted worst first. A player here has both a low fWAR% (poor output vs peers) and low league rating. Consider dropping or trading before their value falls further.
Add targets (New Dashboard)
Top free agents for your two weakest positional groups, ranked by fWAR. Adding a high-fWAR FA at a position where you are below the league average gives the biggest improvement per roster move.
Team diagnostic — formulas and calculations

The Team Diagnostic card appears at the top of the Dashboard and classifies your team using four inputs, derives an archetype from a rule cascade, computes an expected score and trust score, and exposes the pairwise disagreements between signals so you can see exactly which signals are pulling each other apart.

Recent changes
Added ROS% as a 4th forward-looking input. Replaced the 27-cell archetype lookup with a 15-rule continuous cascade that includes a borderline detector. Switched the input pill colors from hard buckets to a smooth red→amber→green gradient. Added a Pairwise gaps section showing the three signal disagreements that drive the trust score.
The four inputs
Team production · fWAR% (0–100)
For each positional group (IF, OF, SP, RP), average the fWAR% of all rostered players. Sum the four group averages. Rank all 10 teams and convert to a 0–100 percentile. Backward-looking — what your team has actually produced this season.
Underlying talent · Rating% (0–100)
Same approach as fWAR% but using league-wide player ratings (talent valuation). Sum group averages, rank teams 0–100. Measures roster quality independent of current performance.
New
Projected production · ROS% (0–100)
Sum each rostered player's raw Team ROS SGP (FanGraphs ROS projection × team rate-stat context × playing-time trust factor) for every team in the league, then rank percentiles. Multipliers are flat — this measures absolute projected fantasy value, not need-fit. Players below the playing-time threshold (10 AB / 5 IP) are filtered out so dynasty stashes don't inflate the number. Forward-looking — what your team is projected to do for the rest of the season.
Luck factor · JOE (−1 to +1)
Season average JOE score from the JOE Factor page. Negative = unlucky, positive = lucky. Buckets: Lucky ≥ +0.12 · Neutral · Unlucky ≤ −0.12.
Pill colors and labels

Each input value gets a Low / Mid / High bucket label (the categorical pill text uses thresholds 45 and 65). The color of the pill, however, is now a continuous interpolation across the input range — so a value at 44 isn't a hard red and a value at 46 isn't a hard amber. The visual transition is smooth across red → amber → green with anchor points at 30 (pure red), 55 (pure amber), and 80 (pure green).

This means a team near a bucket boundary won't visually flip overnight from a small input change. The label is the discrete identity; the color is the magnitude.

Derived scores
Expected Score
expScore = 0.40 × fWAR% + 0.20 × Rating% + 0.30 × ROS% − 10 × JOE
Higher = stronger team. Production weighted highest (current points are real), ROS second (forward-looking value), Rating smaller (since ROS already encodes most talent signal). Interpretation: 70+ True Contender · 55–69 Playoff Caliber · 45–54 Middling · <45 Weak Team. Falls back to the legacy 0.55 × fWAR% + 0.30 × Rating% − 10 × JOE when ROS data isn't loaded.
Trust Score (0–100)
avgGap = avg of |fWAR − Rating|, |fWAR − ROS|, |Rating − ROS|
trust = 100 − (0.8 × avgGap) − (35 × |JOE|)
Penalised by the average disagreement across all three signal pairs and by extreme luck. When all three signals point the same direction, trust is high. When any pair diverges, trust drops. Capped at 80 for teams below average across all three percentiles. Tiers: 80+ Very High · 65+ High · 50+ Medium · 35+ Low · <35 Very Low.
Pairwise gaps
Three signed differences shown directly on the card so you can see what's pulling trust:
  • fWAR − Rating · production vs the talent on the roster
  • fWAR − ROS · current pace vs projection (highlighted — this is the most actionable gap)
  • Rating − ROS · market consensus vs projection
Gaps within ±8 are treated as "aligned" and rendered neutral. Larger gaps render in red/amber and carry an interpretive caption ("projecting upward — current pace below ROS", etc.).
Archetype — rule cascade

When ROS data is loaded, the archetype label is picked by walking a 15-rule cascade against the four inputs. The first rule that matches wins. The cascade replaces the legacy 27-cell ARCHETYPE_MAP lookup, which had hard cliffs at every bucket boundary.

Lucky Contender · fWAR≥65, ROS≥65, JOE≥0.12
True Contender · fWAR≥65, ROS≥65, neutral luck
Selling Window · fWAR≥65, ROS<45
Hot Streak · fWAR≥65, ROS=Mid, lucky
Solid Performer · fWAR≥65, ROS=Mid
Sleeping Giant · fWAR<45, ROS≥65
Unlucky Mid · fWAR<45, ROS=Mid, unlucky
Talent Trap · fWAR<45, Rating≥65
Rebuild · fWAR<45, ROS<45, Rating<45
Rising · fWAR=Mid, ROS≥65
Fading · fWAR=Mid, ROS<45
Lucky Mid · fWAR=Mid, ROS=Mid, lucky
Unlucky Mid · fWAR=Mid, ROS=Mid, unlucky
Middle of the Pack · fWAR=Mid, ROS=Mid
Mixed Signals · fallback

If ROS data isn't loaded, the card falls back to the legacy 27-cell ARCHETYPE_MAP indexed by fWAR-bucket / Rating-bucket / JOE-bucket. See the Team Archetype tab for that table.

Borderline detection

After the rule cascade picks an archetype, the code re-runs the cascade with each input perturbed by ±4 points (just past the 3-point boundary window). If the perturbation produces a different archetype, that name is appended to the caption as "· borderline with X". So a team with fWAR=43 (just below the Low/Mid cutoff at 45) and ROS=70 might show as Sleeping Giant · borderline with Rising — telling you the call could swing with a small input change. Teams firmly inside their archetype get no borderline suffix.

Trend outlook — calculation steps
Step 1 — Trend Strength = |fWAR − ROS| + (10 × |JOE|) when ROS is available; falls back to |fWAR − Rating| + (10 × |JOE|) otherwise
Step 2 — Classify strength: Strong ≥ 20 · Moderate ≥ 10 · Weak < 10
Step 3 — Direction comes from the archetype rule that fired (Up / Slight Up / Flat / Slight Down / Down)
Step 4 — Combine: Strong Positive → "Strong positive outlook" · Moderate Positive → "Positive outlook" · Slight Positive → "Mild positive outlook" · Flat → "Stable outlook" · and mirrored negatives

Switching from |fWAR − Rating| to |fWAR − ROS| as the trend driver is meaningful: it directly answers "are current results above or below where they should end up?" Rating gap conflates two different things (current pace vs talent valuation); ROS gap is the cleaner signal.

Recommended action — logic
Primary move
Contender + Up → Hold / Buy
Contender + Flat → Optimize
Down direction → Sell High / Rebalance
Middle archetype → Choose Direction
Weak/Rebuild → Rebuild
Do not do
JOE < −0.4 → Don't sell core pieces
JOE > +0.4 → Don't chase current production
|fWAR − Rating| > 15 → Don't ignore category imbalance
Otherwise → Avoid unnecessary churn
Best upgrade type
Rating% > fWAR% → High-impact upgrade available
Pitching weaker than hitting → Bullpen / ratio SP
Hitting weaker than pitching → Power bat or SB specialist
ROS% > fWAR% → Buy-low signal
JOE Factor - Scheduling Luck

JOE Factor answers: "If you had played every other team each week, what would your average win% have been - and how does your actual record compare?"

How It's Calculated
Step 1 - Pairwise H2H: For each team each week, simulate a head-to-head matchup vs every other team. Compare all 10 categories. ERA/WHIP are lower-is-better.
Step 2 - Avg Case W%: Average your win% if you'd played all 9 opponents. This is your "deserved" performance.
Step 3 - Luck z-score: luck_z = (Actual W% − Avg Case W%) / std(differences across all teams that week)
Step 4 - JOE Factor: Min-max normalize luck z-scores to [−1, +1] across all teams. +1 = luckiest, −1 = unluckiest.
Charts
Whisker Plot - Each team's vertical range from Worst to Best case. Dots: dark green = Best, gold = Avg, green = Actual, red = Worst.
JOE Bar Chart - Green bars = lucky, red bars = unlucky.
H2H Score Matrix - What the score would have been between every pair of teams. ★ = your actual opponent that week.
Season Avg tab - Averages all metrics across every completed week. This is the source of the Dashboard luck tile.
Player Data - Metric Reference

The Player Data page surfaces four key metrics for every player. Here's what each one means:

Metric What It Measures
League Rating How a player's overall performance stacks up against all players at their type (all hitters or all pitchers), adjusted for sample size.
Pos Rating How strong a player is relative to others at their specific position, highlighting positional advantage or scarcity.
fWAR The total value a player has produced this season above a replacement-level option, based on your league's scoring categories.
fWAR % The player's percentile rank in fWAR within their pool (all hitters, all SP, or all RP), showing how their production compares to peers in the league.
fWAR - Fantasy Wins Above Replacement

fWAR estimates how many fantasy wins a player contributes above a freely available replacement-level player at their position. Higher = more valuable. Configure the model on the Models page.

fWAR Calculation Flow
Step 1 - Z-Score each stat
For every rostered player, each scoring category is z-scored against all rostered players in their broad position group (Infield, Outfield, SP, RP).
z = (player_stat − group_mean) / group_std
ERA and WHIP are negated so lower = better.
Step 2 - Weighted Total Z
Z-scores are summed across all relevant categories, each scaled by its category weight (0–1, adjustable in the Models page).
totalZ = Σ (z_cat × weight_cat)
Step 3 - Playing Time Weight
Total Z is scaled by how much a player has played relative to the average rostered player at their role - capped at 1.0 (never penalises players for playing too much).
ptWeight = min(1.0, playerIP / avgRosteredIP)
Hitters use AB. Pitchers use IP - computed separately for SP and RP so closers are compared to other closers, not penalised by starters who accumulate IP much faster. A closer at 10 IP is compared against the average rostered RP's IP total, not the SP average.
Step 4 - Replacement Level
The Nth-ranked player at each position defines replacement level - the baseline of a freely available player. N is the positional replacement rank (configurable in Models). A lower N = harder position to fill = higher bar.
Step 5 - fWAR
fWAR = playerTotalZ − replacementLevelZ
Positive = above replacement. Negative = below replacement. A +2.0 fWAR player contributes roughly 2 wins above what you'd get by picking up a free agent.
Positional Scarcity Presets
⚾ Pitcher Heavy - SP:147, RP:63. Values pitching depth.
⚖️ Hybrid (default) - SP:123, RP:53, OF:85. Balanced.
🏏 Hitter Heavy - SP:98, OF:100. Values hitting depth.
🎯 Optimal - Weights derived from a scipy optimizer maximizing Spearman rank correlation between team fWAR and points standings across 50 historical team-seasons (2021–2025). Spearman r is shown on the button.
Important: fWAR uses cumulative season stats
fWAR reflects how a player has performed so far this season relative to their peers - it is not a projection. Use the SGP Engine (Player Trade & Market) for forward-looking player value.
fWAR % - Percentile Column

The fWAR % column on the Player Data page converts a player's raw fWAR into a 0–100 percentile rank within their pool of rostered players. It answers: "How does this player's fWAR compare to everyone else currently rostered?"

Hitters
All rostered IF + OF in one pool
SP
All rostered SP in one pool
RP
All rostered RP in one pool
Why separate SP and RP? SPs and RPs have fundamentally different workloads and fWAR scales. A closer with 15 saves and a 2.50 ERA shouldn't be compared against a SP with 180 IP - they're contributing in different ways.
Why rostered only? The percentile measures your standing among active competition - the players other managers are actually deploying. FA players vary wildly in quality and would dilute the signal.
How to use it: A hitter in the 80th percentile is more valuable than 80% of all rostered hitters by fWAR. Sort by fWAR % descending to quickly identify the most impactful players in the league - useful for trade targets and waiver wire decisions.
Team Roster Radar — what "Team fWAR" actually showsPer-category breakdown across the rostered players

The radar charts on the Team Roster page show three lines per category — Team Pos Rating, Team fWAR, and Team ROS SGP. Each is a 0-100 percentile showing how this team ranks against the other 9 in that category. The Team fWAR line is built from per-player z-scores and is more nuanced than it sounds.

How the Team fWAR per-cat line is computed
team_score[cat] = Σ (player.z[cat] × player.ptWeight × cat_weight)

Sums each rostered player's per-category z-score (from the FWAR model), weighted by their playing-time fraction and the league's fWAR category weight. Then teams are ranked league-wide for that category and the rank is converted to a 1-99 percentile: best team gets 99, worst gets 1, others spread evenly.

Reading example

If team WOO shows 77 on the HR axis, that means: WOO ranks 3rd of 10 teams in HR-derived fWAR contribution from rostered players. The conversion: percentile 77 → idx ≈ (99-77)/98 × 9 ≈ 2 → 3rd place.

Why this differs from "77th percentile of HR totals"

It's not "77th percentile of all teams' HR totals." It's "77th percentile of the player-fWAR-z-score sum for HR among teams." The distinction matters:

  • A team with a few HR studs (high individual z-scores) playing every day will score high.
  • A team with many average HR contributors might rank similarly in standings totals but lower here.
  • The metric weights by playing time and league weight, so a concentrated-power roster outperforms a "spread the HR around" roster with the same team total.
Three similarly-named things to keep straight
Name What it shows Per-cat?
Team fWAR donut Sum of p.fWAR by group (IF/OF/SP/RP) No — group totals
Player Data: fWAR% column League-relative percentile of a single player's overall fWAR vs others in their pool No — overall
Radar "Team fWAR" line Team's per-cat z-score sum, ranked vs league Yes — per category

The radar line uses the per-category z-scores under the hood (the p.z[cat] values that feed INTO computing fWAR), not the fWAR number itself. fWAR alone is one number per player; the radar needs 5 axes per side, so it builds from the per-cat z-score components.

Player Ratings - Pos Rating & League Rating

The Player Data page shows two rating columns alongside fWAR. Both are 0–100 percentile scores but measure different things.

POS RATING
How good is this player at their specific position?

A catcher is rated only against other catchers. A shortstop only against shortstops. Multi-position players take their best rating across all eligible positions.
LEAGUE RATING
How good is this player vs all hitters or all pitchers?

All hitters compete in one pool. SP in one pool, RP in another. A 90 League Rating means the player outperforms 90% of all rostered players at their type.
Rating Calculation Flow
Step 1 - Build position pools
All rostered players are indexed by fine position: C, 1B, 2B, 3B, SS, OF, SP, RP. Multi-position players appear in multiple pools.
Step 2 - Percentile each category with sample-size weighting
For each scoring category, the player's percentile rank within their pool is computed. Rate stats (AVG, ERA, WHIP) are downweighted based on sample size:
Hitters (AB)
0–49 → 0.2×
50–99 → 0.4×
100–149 → 0.6×
150+ → 1.0×
SP (IP)
0–9 → 0.2×
10–19 → 0.4×
20–34 → 0.6×
35–49 → 0.8×
50+ → 1.0×
RP (IP): 0–4 → 0.2× · 5–9 → 0.4× · 10–14 → 0.6× · 15–24 → 0.8× · 25+ → 1.0×
Step 3 - Weighted average → 0–100 score
rating = Σ(percentile_cat × factor_cat) / Σ(factor_cat)
Multi-pos players keep the best rating across all eligible positions as their Pos Rating.
Rating Color Scale
65+ Elite
50–64 Above Avg
35–49 Below Avg
<35 Poor
Player ArchetypesCombining fWAR%, Pos Rating & League Rating

Every player is assigned an archetype based on the relationship between their three key metrics. This surfaces actionable insights - whether a player is a keeper, a sell-high, or a hidden gem.

Thresholds
Metric High Mid Low
fWAR% ≥ 65 45–64 < 45
ROS% ≥ 65 45–64 < 45
League Rating ≥ 65 45–64 < 45
Recent changes
Added two new sortable columns to the Player Data table — Curr SGP and ROS SGP — sitting between League Rating and Archetype. Added a ROS Outlook tile (9th profile card) that shows projected SGP, Δ vs current pace, per-category contribution mini-chart, and a Breakout / Regression / Stable signal. Replaced the previous 9 player archetypes with a new 9-rule cascade that incorporates ROS data — giving each archetype a forward-looking dimension. The legacy 9-rule cascade still runs as a fallback when ROS data isn't loaded.
True Elite
fWAR ≥ 65 · ROS ≥ 65 · Lg ≥ 65
Production, projection, and talent all elite.
Untouchable cornerstone
Sell High
fWAR ≥ 65 · ROS < 45
Producing now but ROS projects regression.
Trade while value is up
Format Mismatch Star
fWAR ≥ 65 · Lg < 45
Producing here, but talent valuation lower league-wide.
Hold / slight sell-high
Overperformer
fWAR ≥ 65 · ROS Mid · Lg Mid
Output above what projection or talent supports.
Sell-high
Breakout Candidate
fWAR Mid · ROS ≥ 65
Average results but ROS projects upward.
Buy-low / hold
Buy-Low
fWAR < 45 · ROS ≥ 65
Down right now, but ROS projects rebound.
Buy-low
Underperforming Stud
fWAR < 45 · Lg ≥ 65
Talent says yes, results haven't shown it.
Buy-low
Stable Contributor
balanced fallback
Balanced across signals — reliable, not elite.
Hold
Replacement Level
fWAR < 45 · ROS < 45 · Lg < 45
Weak across all three signals.
Drop / ignore
How to use archetypes
The most actionable signals come from gaps between the three inputs. fWAR ≥ 65 + ROS < 45 means a Sell High candidate — currently producing, projected to fall. fWAR < 45 + ROS ≥ 65 means a Buy-Low candidate — down right now but projected to rebound. League Rating ≥ 65 + fWAR < 45 means an Underperforming Stud — talent says yes, results haven't shown it. Click any player row on the Player Data page to see their full breakdown including the new ROS Outlook tile (rightmost).
Player Profile Radar ChartHow the three lines are calculated

When you click a player row on the Player Data page, a radar chart appears showing three overlapping lines across the five scoring categories. All three lines use the same 0–100 scale where 50 = league average, so they can be directly compared.

fWAR% (Yellow)
Per-category z-score percentile vs all rostered players in the same pool (all hitters, SP, or RP). Incorporates fWAR model weights - categories your league values more score higher.
Pos Rating (Mint)
Per-category raw stat percentile vs position peers only (e.g. SS vs SS). Sample-size weighted. Shows where this player excels or lags relative to others at their position.
League Rating (Blue)
Per-category raw stat percentile vs all rostered hitters or pitchers. Sample-size weighted. Shows the player's absolute strength in each category across the whole league.
Calculation Steps
fWAR% line - z-score percentile
For each category, the player's fw.z[cat] is ranked against all rostered players in the same pool. below / total → 0–1 → × 100. ERA and WHIP z-scores are already negated in the fWAR model (lower ERA = higher z-score), so all categories are treated as higher = better - no additional inversion needed.
Pos Rating & League Rating lines - stat percentile
For each category, the raw stat is percentile-ranked within the relevant pool. The raw percentile is centred: dev = (pct − 0.5) × sampleFactor, then re-centred at 0.5 for display: v = dev + 0.5 → × 100.
Key difference between the three lines
fWAR% uses z-scores (which bake in your league's category weights), while Pos Rating and League Rating use raw stats. A player might score high in fWAR% for SB because SB is scarce, even if their raw SB total looks modest - this divergence is intentional and signals fantasy-specific value.
Reading the chart: When fWAR% (yellow) sits above League Rating (blue) in a category, the player is generating more fantasy value in that category than their raw stats suggest - often due to category scarcity or weight. When it sits below, the raw stat is strong but not converting to fantasy value.
Curr SGP & ROS SGP columnsNew sortable columns in the Player Data table

Two new sortable columns sit between League Rating and Archetype, both showing raw player SGP (no team-context multipliers, no need-weighting). Sort by either to surface specific player profiles — high Curr / low ROS for sell candidates, low Curr / high ROS for buy targets.

CURR SGP
SGP from this season's stats only. Backward-looking — what the player has actually produced. Calculated against a neutral 4000-AB / 1300-IP league pool at .260 / 3.90 / 1.20 baselines so values are intrinsic to the player, not skewed by any one team's context.
ROS SGP
SGP from FantasyPros rest-of-season projections. Forward-looking — what the player is projected to produce. Same neutral-pool calculation as Curr SGP for direct comparison.
counting_sgp = stat / SGP_denominator
avg_sgp = (newAVG − poolAVG) / 0.008 × min(1, AB / 350)
era_sgp = (poolERA − newERA) / 0.106
whip_sgp = (poolWHIP − newWHIP) / 0.0191

total_sgp = Σ catSGP × trust_factor

Trust = min(1, AB/250) for hitters, min(1, IP/60) for SP, min(1, IP/30) for RP. AVG additionally applies the 350-AB sub-trust to dampen rate-stat noise on partial-season players.

ROS Outlook tile9th profile card · click any player row

When you click a player row, a new ROS Outlook tile appears at the right end of the profile panel (with a green NEW ribbon). It packs four pieces of forward-looking information into the standard 250×250 tile.

Headline ROS SGP — large green number, color tier matches the table cell color (≥2.0 dark green, ≥0.5 light green, ≥−0.5 neutral, < −0.5 red).
Curr SGP + Δ vs ROS — two small tiles below the headline. The Δ is signed (ROS − Curr) and color-coded: ≥+0.5 green (improving), ≥−0.5 neutral, < −0.5 amber (regressing).
5-bar mini-chart — per-category ROS SGP contribution (R/HR/RBI/SB/AVG for hitters, W/K/SVH3/ERA/WHIP for pitchers). Bar height shows magnitude relative to the player's largest contributor; color shows direction (green positive, red negative, gray near zero).
Signal callout — at the bottom, a one-line read on the Δ: ↑ Breakout signal when ROS exceeds Curr by ≥1.0 SGP, ↓ Regression risk when Curr exceeds ROS by ≥1.0 SGP, = Stable otherwise.
Correlations

Shows how strongly each category correlates with fantasy points standings - tells you what's actually driven winning in this league.

Two Tabs
2026 Season - 10 data points (one per team). Quick signal but noisy at this sample size.
Historical 2021-2025 - 50 data points (10 teams × 5 seasons). Much more reliable. Use this to guide your roster strategy.
Pearson r Interpretation
|r| ≥ 0.7 - Strong. This category has consistently driven standings. Chase it.
|r| 0.4–0.7 - Moderate. Meaningful but not decisive.
|r| < 0.2 - Weak. Deprioritize. Winning this category hasn't historically translated to standings.

ERA and WHIP signs are inverted (lower = better) so positive r means better ERA = more points.

Team Positional StrengthDashboard heat map

The Dashboard heat map shows how strong your team is at each broad position group compared to the rest of the league. It answers: "Where am I strong, and where do I need to upgrade?"

How Team Positional Strength is Calculated
Step 1 - Compute each player's Pos Rating
Every rostered player gets a 0–100 Pos Rating - their percentile vs all other players at the same fine position (C vs C, SS vs SS, etc.). See the Player Ratings section for the full methodology.
Step 2 - Map fine positions to broad groups
Each player's best fine position maps up to one of four broad groups for team aggregation:
Infield
C · 1B · 2B · 3B · SS
Outfield
OF
SP
SP
RP
RP
Step 3 - Average Pos Ratings per group per team
For each team, the Pos Ratings of all players in each broad group are averaged. A team with three strong shortstops and weak catchers will show a mid-range Infield score - it's an average across all infield positions.
team_group_score = avg(Pos Rating of all players in group)
Step 4 - Color the heat map
Each cell is colored by the team's average group score vs the league:
≥ 65
Elite
52–65
Strong
30–42
Weak
< 30
Poor
How to use it: A red Infield tile + green SP tile = sell pitching depth for infield help. Cross-reference with the SGP By Position view to find specific upgrade targets at the weak position.
SGP By Position - Upgrade Finder

The Player Trade & Market → Hitter/Pitcher SGP → By Position view combines positional strength with SGP to find specific upgrades. For each position slot it shows your current best starter vs the best available upgrade, with a delta SGP verdict:

🔥 UPGRADE (Δ > 0.7)
↑ Consider (Δ > 0.3)
✓ Fine
Data Pipeline

All data is fetched by fetch_fantrax.py, which runs as a GitHub Action and saves JSON files to the data/ directory.

Data Files
standings.jsonFantrax league standings + scoring period weekly stats
standings_season.jsonSeason standings by category with individual category tables
players_hitting/pitching.jsonAll hitter/pitcher stats from Fantrax
full_schedule.jsonComplete 20-period schedule [{period, away, home}]
fangraphs_hitting/pitching.jsonBaseball Savant Statcast leaderboard data
targets_hitting/pitching.jsonBuy-low/sell-high signal data
optimal_fwar_weights.jsonScipy-optimized positional weights + Spearman r
Triggering a Data Refresh

Go to your GitHub repo → Actions → the fetch workflow → Run workflow. Data typically takes 2–3 minutes to update. The site reads from GitHub Pages so changes are live within seconds of the Action completing.

Required Secrets

Set in GitHub repo Settings → Secrets: FANTRAX_USERNAME, FANTRAX_PASSWORD, FANTRAX_FX_RM, FANTRAX_CF_CLEARANCE. The CF_CLEARANCE cookie expires periodically and needs to be refreshed manually.

Glossary
fWAR - Fantasy Wins Above Replacement. How many fantasy wins a player contributes above a freely available replacement at their position.
SGP - Standings Gain Points. How many standings positions a player's projected stats would gain your team. Based on the average gap between adjacent teams in each category.
JOE Factor - Scheduling luck metric. Compares your actual win% to the average win% you'd have gotten if you'd played every team. Normalized to [−1, +1].
xwOBA - Expected weighted on-base average. Measures quality of contact (exit velocity + launch angle) to estimate what a hitter's wOBA "should" be.
wOBA - Weighted on-base average. Like OBP but weights each outcome (walk, single, HR etc.) by its actual run value.
xERA - Expected ERA. What a pitcher's ERA "should" be based on quality of contact allowed, independent of defense and luck.
BABIP - Batting Average on Balls In Play. A high BABIP often indicates luck; a low BABIP can signal bad luck. Typical range: .280–.320.
Barrel% - Percentage of batted balls hit with ideal exit velocity (95+ mph) and launch angle (26–30°). Strong predictor of future HR and xwOBA.
Hard Hit% - Percentage of batted balls hit 95+ mph. General measure of contact quality.
Z-Score - How far above or below average a value is, measured in standard deviations. Z = (value − mean) / std. Used throughout the site for cross-category comparisons.
Pearson r - Correlation coefficient from −1 to +1. Measures how linearly related two variables are. |r| ≥ 0.7 = strong, 0.4–0.7 = moderate, < 0.2 = weak.
Spearman r - Like Pearson r but based on rank order rather than raw values. More robust to outliers. Used in the fWAR optimizer.
Replacement Level - The performance of a freely available player (waiver wire / end of bench). fWAR measures value above this baseline, not above zero.
SVH3 - Saves + Holds × 3-point value. Your league's combined saves/holds scoring category.
ROS - Rest of season. Projections scaled by current pace: ROS = current_stat × (remaining_games / games_played).
ChangelogUpdates from April 9, 2026 onward
APR 9, 2026 Initial Changelog Entry
NEWSite Guide page with Getting Started, all page guides, glossary, and changelog
NEWJOE Factor Season Avg tab with whisker plot and JOE bar chart
NEWTrade Waiver Wire Targets - Pitchers tab (xERA, BABIP YoY, K%, Velo)
NEWQuadrant chart on Trade Waiver Wire Targets with dropdown color modes (Quadrant, Fantasy Team, Hard Hit%, Barrel%)
NEWSGP By Position view - shows your starter vs best available upgrade at each slot
NEWHistorical Correlations tab (50 team-seasons, 2021-2025) with strategic callout
NEWOptimal fWAR preset - scipy L-BFGS-B optimizer maximizing Spearman r
NEWMatch Predictions augmented with standings points (60% category H2H + 40% pts sigmoid)
IMPROVEDJOE Factor tabs: pill-style navigation, content swaps in place (no page scrolling)
IMPROVEDSGP values fixed - rate stats (AVG/ERA/WHIP) were inflating to 1000s due to incorrect AB/IP scaling
IMPROVEDSGP personalized to your standings: categories you're losing get up to 2.5× weight
IMPROVEDDashboard: Top 5 fWAR moved above trade suggestions, column alignment fixed, matchup period detection fixed
IMPROVEDLeague Leaders Team section now reads from individual category tables in standings_season.json
FIXEDfWAR-W column removed from fWAR page, Roster page, and League Leaders
FIXEDSVH3 standardized throughout (previously mixed SV/SVH3 references causing missing data)
FIXEDDashboard no longer hangs - removed infinite wait loop for optional data sources
FIXEDHistoric League Data SGP column removed from per-year tables

Future entries will be added here. Updated approximately weekly.

Team Archetype — ROS-aware 4-input modelActive when ROS projections are loaded
What's new
The Team Archetype model now uses four inputs instead of three. The legacy 3-input model (fWAR% / Rating% / JOE) remains as a fallback when ROS data hasn't loaded, but when available, the 4-input ROS-aware cascade takes over and produces more forward-looking archetype calls.

The upgraded model classifies your team using fWAR% (current production), ROS% (forward-looking projection), Rating% (roster talent), and JOE (schedule luck). Combining current results with future projection lets the cascade distinguish "currently great but projected to fade" from "genuinely elite" — a distinction the legacy model couldn't make.

The new ROS% input
For each rostered player who passes the playing-time filter, look up their raw Team ROS SGP (player projected SGP, no need-weights). Sum across the team. Then rank all 10 teams — your percentile rank is the team's ROS%.
ROS% = percentile_rank( Σ player_ROS_SGP across rostered players )
15-rule ROS-aware cascade

Buckets are the same 45 / 65 thresholds as the legacy model: High ≥ 65, Mid 45–64, Low < 45. JOE buckets stay Lucky / Neutral / Unlucky at ±0.12. Cascade fires the first matching rule top-to-bottom:

Trigger condition Archetype Direction Action
fwH & roH & luckyLucky ContenderSlight DownSell High
fwH & roHTrue ContenderFlatHold / Optimize
fwH & roLSelling WindowDownSell High
fwH & roM & luckyHot StreakSlight DownSell High
fwH & roMSolid PerformerSlight DownOptimize
fwL & roHSleeping GiantUpHold / Buy
fwL & roM & unluckyUnlucky MidSlight UpHold
fwL & rtHTalent TrapSlight UpHold / Reassess
fwL & roL & rtLRebuildFlatRebuild
fwM & roHRisingUpHold / Buy
fwM & roLFadingDownSell High
fwM & roM & luckyLucky MidSlight DownOptimize / Sell
fwM & roM & unluckyUnlucky MidSlight UpHold / Buy small
fwM & roMMiddle of the PackFlatChoose Direction
(default fallback)Mixed SignalsFlatReassess
The key new distinctions ROS adds
  • True Contender vs Selling Window: both have high fWAR%, but ROS distinguishes "elite that will stay elite" from "currently great but headed down."
  • Sleeping Giant: low fWAR% but high ROS% — the buy-low signal the legacy model couldn't see directly.
  • Rising vs Fading: same mid-tier fWAR%, but opposite trajectories on ROS.
  • Solid Performer vs Hot Streak: high fWAR% with mid ROS%; the JOE input separates "you've earned it" from "luck inflated it."
Borderline detection

After classifying, the cascade re-runs with each input perturbed ±3 around the bucket boundaries (45 / 65). If a different archetype would fire, the diagnostic appends a "borderline" note showing the alternate calls. Helps the user understand when the team is at a tipping point between archetypes vs firmly in one.

Trust score (model confidence)
With four inputs, trust is computed from the average of the three pairwise gaps:
Trust = avg(|fwarPct − rosPct|, |fwarPct − lgPct|, |rosPct − lgPct|)
Lower gaps = higher trust (signals agree). Higher gaps = lower trust (signals diverge, archetype call is less stable).
Fallback behavior

When ROS data hasn't loaded yet (cold start, first session), the diagnostic falls back to the legacy 3-input model documented in the next card. The same 27-cell archetype map applies. Once ROS data lands, the diagnostic re-renders with the 4-input cascade output.

Legacy 3-input model — fallback when ROS unavailableUsed when FantasyPros data hasn't loaded

Every team is classified into one of 27 archetypes based on three model inputs: fWAR% (actual fantasy production), Rating% (underlying roster talent), and JOE Factor (schedule luck). Each input is bucketed independently, and the three-bucket combination produces a unique key, a recommended direction, and an action.

Step 1 — fWAR% (Team Production Percentile)
For each positional group (Infield, Outfield, SP, RP), compute the average fWAR percentile of all rostered players. Average the four group scores to get the team total. Rank all 10 teams — your percentile rank (0–100) is your fWAR%.
fWAR% = percentile_rank( mean( avg_fWAR_pct(IF), avg_fWAR_pct(OF), avg_fWAR_pct(SP), avg_fWAR_pct(RP) ) )
Step 2 — Rating% (Underlying Talent Percentile)
Same approach as fWAR%, but uses each player's league rating (positional rank vs the full player pool) rather than fWAR percentile. Captures roster quality independent of recent production — a team with elite talent but underperforming players will show High Rating% but Low fWAR%.
Rating% = percentile_rank( mean( avg_league_rating(IF), avg_league_rating(OF), avg_league_rating(SP), avg_league_rating(RP) ) )
Step 3 — JOE Factor (Schedule Luck)
Measures scheduling luck on a −1.0 to +1.0 scale. Positive = you've faced weaker opponents; negative = unlucky scheduling. See the JOE Factor tab for the full formula.
Step 4 — Bucket each input
fWAR% and Rating%
≥ 65 → High
45–64 → Mid
< 45 → Low
JOE Factor
≥ +0.12 → Lucky
−0.12 to +0.12 → Neutral
≤ −0.12 → Unlucky
Step 5 — Archetype Key
Concatenate the three bucket labels to form the key:
Key = {fWAR% bucket} – {Rating% bucket} – {JOE bucket}

Example: fWAR% = 72 → High, Rating% = 58 → Mid, JOE = −0.18 → Unlucky
Key = High-Mid-Unlucky → Real Contender · Direction: Up · Action: Hold / Buy
Trust Score — Model Confidence
Measures how reliable the archetype is (0–100). High = fWAR% and Rating% are aligned and JOE is near zero. Low = large production/talent gap or extreme luck values distorting results.

Trust = 100 − (0.8 × |fWAR% − Rating%|) − (35 × |JOE|)
Capped at 80 if both fWAR% < 50 and Rating% < 50. Clamped to 0–100.
65–100
High / Very High
Model alignment strong
50–64
Medium
Moderate confidence
0–49
Low
High noise — use caution
All 27 Archetypes
fWAR Bucket Rating Bucket JOE Bucket Key Archetype Direction Action
High High Unlucky High-High-Unlucky Underperforming Contender Up Hold / Buy
High High Neutral High-High-Neutral True Contender Stable Push In
High High Lucky High-High-Lucky Strong but Inflated Contender Slight Down Hold / Trim Depth
High Mid Unlucky High-Mid-Unlucky Real Contender Up Hold / Buy
High Mid Neutral High-Mid-Neutral Good Contender Stable Optimize
High Mid Lucky High-Mid-Lucky Overperforming Contender Down Rebalance
High Low Unlucky High-Low-Unlucky Format-Favored Team Slight Up Patch Weaknesses
High Low Neutral High-Low-Neutral Format Mismatch Winner Flat Monitor
High Low Lucky High-Low-Lucky Fragile Contender Down Sell High / Rebalance
Mid High Unlucky Mid-High-Unlucky Hidden Sleeper Up Buy / Stay Patient
Mid High Neutral Mid-High-Neutral Underperforming Good Team Up Rebalance
Mid High Lucky Mid-High-Lucky Talent-Rich but Uneven Flat Improve Fit
Mid Mid Unlucky Mid-Mid-Unlucky Stable Team with Upside Up Hold / Small Adds
Mid Mid Neutral Mid-Mid-Neutral True Middle Team Flat Choose Direction
Mid Mid Lucky Mid-Mid-Lucky Soft Middle Team Down Sell High / Rebalance
Mid Low Unlucky Mid-Low-Unlucky Scrappy Team Slight Up Short-Term Adds
Mid Low Neutral Mid-Low-Neutral Limited Ceiling Team Flat Rebalance
Mid Low Lucky Mid-Low-Lucky Pretender Down Sell High
Low High Unlucky Low-High-Unlucky Hidden Sleeper Up Be Patient / Buy
Low High Neutral Low-High-Neutral Misbuilt Good Team Flat Fix Category Fit
Low High Lucky Low-High-Lucky Good Talent, Bad Fit Down Rebuild Structure
Low Mid Unlucky Low-Mid-Unlucky Weak Team with Bad Luck Slight Up Incremental Fixes
Low Mid Neutral Low-Mid-Neutral Weak-Middle Team Flat Rebuild or Churn
Low Mid Lucky Low-Mid-Lucky False Middle Team Down Sell Hot Pieces
Low Low Unlucky Low-Low-Unlucky Rebuild Candidate Slight Up Rebuild
Low Low Neutral Low-Low-Neutral Weak Team Flat Rebuild
Low Low Lucky Low-Low-Lucky False Hope Team Down Sell / Rebuild
ROS Projections — How the rankings work

The ROS page ranks all projected players by their expected value added to a specific team. The final score combines raw projected stats, SGP value, team need weighting, and projection reliability (Trust). Each component is explained below.

Step 1 — Raw ROS SGP per Category
SGP (Standings Gain Points) measures how many standings places a player's projected stats are worth. Each counting stat is divided by the SGP denominator — the average gap between adjacent teams in that category.
Counting Stats (R, HR, RBI, SB, W, K, SVH3)
SGP = ROS_stat / SGP_denominator
Example: 25 projected HR ÷ 21.2 = +1.18 SGP
SGP Denominators
R65.4W15.3
HR21.2K216.8
RBI54.8SVH318.5
SB17.0ERA0.106
AVG0.008WHIP0.0191
Rate Stats — Team Context Required

AVG, ERA, and WHIP depend on your current team totals because adding a player changes the team average. The selected team's season AB and IP are pulled from the Standings - Stat Totals table.

AVG
New_H = Team_H + ROS_H
New_AB = Team_AB + ROS_AB
New_AVG = New_H / New_AB
delta = New_AVG − Team_AVG
AVG_SGP = delta / 0.008
Note: denominator scaled to 0.008 (vs raw SGP gap of ~0.00178) to prevent AVG from dominating total SGP. Elite AVG player ≈ +2 SGP, inline with top counting stat contributors.
ERA
Team_ER = Team_ERA × IP / 9
Player_ER = ROS_ERA × ROS_IP / 9
New_ERA = (Team_ER + Player_ER)
          ÷ (Team_IP + ROS_IP) × 9
delta = Team_ERA − New_ERA
ERA_SGP = delta / 0.106
WHIP
Team_BR = Team_WHIP × IP
Player_BR = ROS_WHIP × ROS_IP
New_WHIP = (Team_BR + Player_BR)
          ÷ (Team_IP + ROS_IP)
delta = Team_WHIP − New_WHIP
WHIP_SGP = delta / 0.0191
Step 2 — Team Need Weighting
Each category SGP is multiplied by a need weight based on how your selected team ranks in that category vs the rest of the league. Rank is determined by the category Z-score from the Z-Scores page. Lower rank = weaker category = higher multiplier.
Weighted_Cat_SGP = Cat_SGP × need_multiplier(team_rank)
×0.5
Rank 1–2
Already elite
×0.8
Rank 3–4
Above average
×1.0
Rank 5–6
Average
×1.4
Rank 7–8
Below average
×1.8
Rank 9–10
Weak — need it
Step 3 — Trust Score
Trust measures how much to rely on the projection. Players with more projected playing time have more reliable projections. Trust is applied as a final multiplier to Total SGP — so a 50% trust player is valued at half their raw SGP.
Hitters
Trust = min(1, ROS_AB / 250)
250+ AB = 100% · 125 AB = 50%
Starting Pitchers
Trust = min(1, ROS_IP / 60)
60+ IP = 100% · 30 IP = 50%
Relievers
Trust = min(1, ROS_IP / 30)
30+ IP = 100% · 15 IP = 50%
AVG sub-trust: The AVG component additionally applies min(1, AB / 350) on top of the standard 250-AB trust. Counting stats (R, HR, RBI, SB) are unaffected. Damping AVG harder for partial-season players prevents projection noise on a single rate stat from dominating Total SGP. See the simulator card below for full details.
Final Score
Total SGP = Σ (Cat_SGP × need_multiplier) × Trust
Players are sorted by Total SGP descending. A player who fills a weak category, has a reliable projection, and has high projected playing time will rank highest. Change the team dropdown to see rankings from any team's perspective.
Add / Drop Simulator — How ROS SGP is calculated

The Add / Drop Simulator on the ROS Predictions page lets you compare any two players head-to-head and see the net swing for the selected team. The headline number on each card and in the comparison table is Team ROS SGP — an estimate of how many standings points that player will contribute to your team over the rest of the season. Same player, different team, different number.

The pipeline has four layers: per-category SGP → need-weight multiplier → sum across categories → trust factor.

Layer 1 — Per-category Raw SGP
For each scoring category, the player's projected stat is divided by the SGP denominator (how much of that stat moves you one standings spot). For rate stats (AVG, ERA, WHIP), the math instead asks “how does adding this player's volume to my team's existing rate stat shift it?” — so it uses your team's running totals.
Counting stats (R, HR, RBI, SB, W, K, SVH3)
catSGP = projectedStat / SGP_D[cat]
70 projected ROS runs ÷ 65.4 = +1.07 SGP in R
Rate stats (AVG / ERA / WHIP)
newH = teamAVG × teamAB + projHits
newAB = teamAB + projAB
catSGP = ((newH/newAB) − teamAVG) / SGP_D.avg
ERA / WHIP follow the same shape using IP and team rate stats.
Layer 2 — Need-weight Multiplier
Each category SGP is multiplied by a team-specific weight. Weak categories get multipliers above 1, strong ones get less. Same +1.07 R for a team already top-1 in runs gets discounted; for a team starving for runs it gets inflated.
weighted = catSGP × (mults[cat] || 1)
This is the value that fills each cell of the comparison table's ROS SGP column. The same multipliers used by the ROS Predictions table (see the previous card for the rank → multiplier mapping) are applied here.
Layer 3 — Sum Across Categories
Sum the weighted category SGPs. Hitters: R, HR, RBI, SB, AVG. Pitchers: W, K, SVH3, ERA, WHIP.
total = Σ weighted catSGP
Layer 4 — Trust Factor
The total is multiplied by a sample-size confidence factor capped at 1.0. Players with limited projected playing time are discounted accordingly.
Hitters
trust = min(1, AB / 250)
100 AB → ×0.40
Starting Pitchers
trust = min(1, IP / 60)
60+ IP → ×1.00
Relievers
trust = min(1, IP / 30)
30+ IP → ×1.00
AVG sub-trust · 350-AB threshold
The AVG component gets an additional sub-trust factor on top of the standard 250-AB trust above:
avgTrust = min(1, AB / 350)
Applied only to the AVG SGP component — counting stats (R, HR, RBI, SB) keep their standard 250-AB trust unchanged. Reasoning: AVG is volume-weighted in team-context math (a player's AB pool gets blended into the team's AB total), and rate-stat projections are noisier than counting-stat projections. The 350-AB threshold dampens AVG's contribution harder for partial-season players.
350+ AB
no change
250 AB
×0.71
150 AB
×0.43
75 AB
×0.21
Final Formula
ROS_SGP = Σ (catSGP × need_multiplier) × trust
Two flavors of “ROS SGP” in the simulator
Raw ROS SGP
Same math, but multipliers set to 1 — pure projection value, no team context. Shown only on the Add card.
Team ROS SGP
The full pipeline above. Shown on both player cards, every cell of the comparison table, the Σ row, and feeds into the Net (Add − Drop).
The Net card always equals Add Team ROS SGP − Drop Team ROS SGP. A positive Net means the swap projects to gain you standings points; a negative Net means it loses them, given your team's current category needs.
Rate-stat warning chip
When the Net SGP verdict is meaningful (|Net| ≥ 0.5) and a single rate stat is doing most of the work, the simulator surfaces an amber warning chip directly under the verdict. The chip identifies the dominant rate stat (AVG for hitters, ERA or WHIP for pitchers) and the percentage of the Net it accounts for.
rateContribution = Σ (addCatSGP − dropCatSGP)
    over rate cats: AVG (hitters) | ERA, WHIP (pitchers)

warn if |rateContribution| ÷ |Net| ≥ 0.70
Example chip: “⚠ Rate-stat heavy. ~85% of this Net SGP comes from AVG. Rate-stat projections are noisier than counting stats — verify before acting.”
Why this matters: AVG, ERA, and WHIP are amplified by the team-context math (a player's full projected AB or IP gets blended into your team's pool, leveraging small projection differences). Combined with high need-weight multipliers when the rate stat is a team weakness, a single 25-point AVG projection difference can produce a "Big Upgrade" verdict by itself. The warning gives you a chance to second-guess before pulling the trigger.
Suggested Moves — How recommendations work

The Suggested Moves card surfaces the top 5 add/drop pairs for the selected team automatically, ranked by need-weighted Net SGP. It pairs every eligible candidate against every droppable rostered player, scores each pair with the same math the simulator uses, and shows the best ones — with one-click loading into the simulator below.

How pairs are generated
For every Add candidate, every droppable rostered player whose positions overlap is considered. Position overlap means the Add player can play at least one position the Drop player covers (so a 1B,DH Add can replace a 1B Drop, but not a SS Drop).
Filters
Position
Restricts both Add and Drop to that position. Pills change between Hitters (C / 1B / 2B / 3B / SS / OF / DH) and Pitchers (SP / RP).
Status
Free Agent = waiver pickups only. Rostered = trade target view (players on other teams). All = both. Players on your selected team are always excluded.
Min Net
Hides marginal swaps. Default +0.25. Threshold applies to whichever score the active Sort by dictates.
Protect top performers
When checked, only the bottom half of your roster (by ROS SGP, per position group) is eligible to be dropped. Stops the algorithm from suggesting drops of your stars.
Sort by category
The Sort by row sets which score the suggestions are ranked by. Total (default) uses full need-weighted Net SGP. Picking a category — R, HR, RBI, SB, AVG for hitters; W, K, SVH3, ERA, WHIP for pitchers — ranks pairs by the Net for that single category.
When a category is active, the Min Net threshold, the Net column, and the verdict pill all switch to that category's score. The category pill itself is colored to match the team's need tier for that cat (red ≈ high need, gray ≈ average, green ≈ already strong), so the visual stays consistent with the Team Need card above.
score = (sortCat === 'TOTAL') ? (Add ROS SGP − Drop ROS SGP) : Cat_Delta(Add) − Cat_Delta(Drop)
The ×multiplier subscript on each category pill (e.g. HR ×1.8) shows the team's current need-weight from the Team Need card. It's informational — clicking a pill sets the sort, not the multiplier.
Reason chips
Each suggestion shows up to 2 chips on the Add side surfacing the largest category gains. When sorting by Total, chips prioritize high-need categories first; when sorting by a category, that category becomes the prominent primary chip and a single secondary chip follows.
Try this & Hide
Try this ↓ loads the Add and Drop into the simulator below for the full per-category breakdown, radar chart, and Net verification. Hide dismisses the pair for the session (cleared on page refresh or team change).
How a pitcher can have negative ROS SGPWhen rates outweigh counting stats

A pitcher's ROS SGP sums contributions from five categories. The counting cats (W, K, SVH3) can only add — they're always non-negative. The rate cats (ERA, WHIP) compare the player against a neutral pool baseline (3.90 ERA / 1.20 WHIP) and can go negative when the player's projected rate is worse than the pool. A bad-rate pitcher's ERA + WHIP penalties can exceed his counting-stat gains, producing a net negative.

Per-category formulas
W      = projected_W   / D2.w
K      = projected_K   / D2.k
SVH3   = projected_SV / D2.svh3
ERA    = (POOL_ERA − blended_ERA)  / D2.era
WHIP   = (POOL_WHIP − blended_WHIP) / D2.whip

When the player's projected ERA > 3.90 (pool baseline), blended_ERA rises above the pool, and the formula returns negative. Same for WHIP > 1.20.

Worked example — Mitch Keller

FP ROS projection: ~80 IP, 4 W, 75 K, 4.40 ERA, 1.35 WHIP

Category Calculation Contribution
W 4 / 15.3 +0.26
K 75 / 216.8 +0.35
SVH3 0 / 18.5 0.00
ERA (3.90 − 3.93) / 0.106 −0.31
WHIP (1.20 − 1.213) / 0.0191 −0.66
Net total ROS SGP −0.36

The +0.61 from W and K isn't enough to offset the −0.97 from ERA and WHIP. Net contribution to your team's standings is negative — meaning he actively hurts your standings position even after accounting for his strikeouts.

What this means in practice

A negative-SGP pitcher is actively hurting your standings. Even a 4-win, 75-K projection isn't enough if his rates dilute team ERA / WHIP. Better to start a streamer or leave the slot empty than activate him in a real start. The archetype cascade catches this — pitchers with high current fWAR but low ROS SGP get tagged "Sell High" — trade them while their value still reflects past performance.

A subtle asymmetry — counting vs rate

Counting stats (R, HR, RBI, SB, W, K, SVH3) can only add to SGP. Rate stats (AVG, ERA, WHIP) can both add and subtract. This produces a clear pattern:

  • A great pitcher (200 K, 2.50 ERA) has both K and ERA pulling up — strongly positive SGP.
  • An average pitcher (150 K, 3.90 ERA) has only K contributing — modestly positive.
  • A bad pitcher (100 K, 4.50 ERA) has K pulling up modestly but ERA pulling down hard — net negative.

Hitters almost never go meaningfully negative because their rate cat (AVG) gets a playing-time sub-trust factor (min(1, AB/350)) that dampens partial-season AVG impact. You'd need a regular starter hitting around .180 to push a hitter's overall SGP negative. Pitchers face no such damping on ERA/WHIP, so negative ROS SGP is far more common on the pitching side.

What the Lineup Organizer does

Daily lineup builder. For each starting slot in your league (C, 1B, 2B, 3B, SS, CI, MI, 5×OF, UT, 9×P), it ranks every eligible player on your roster by their expected SGP contribution today — weighted by whether their team plays, and adjusted for who they're facing. The top eligible player at each slot gets the green "In lineup" tag. Everyone left over lands on the bench row.

What it isn't: the optimizer doesn't know what your actual Fantrax lineup currently is. It generates a recommendation from your full roster every time. "In lineup" means "the optimizer's pick", not "currently starting on Fantrax."

Today-score formulaHitters and pitchers, end-to-end

Each card's big number is the "today-score" — the expected SGP gain from starting this player today. The smaller number below it shows the matchup multiplier (e.g. ×1.09) so you can see exactly how much the opposing pitcher (or lineup) moved the needle. Hidden when there's no adjustment.

HITTERS
// ROS rate (what FP projects forward)
ros_sgp = _rosAdPlayerSGP(p, true)
ros_per_game = ros_sgp / max(1, AB_proj / 3.7)

// Current-season rate (how player is performing now)
cur_sgp = _rosCurrSGP(cs, true)
cur_per_game = cur_sgp / max(1, AB_actual / 3.7)

// 50/50 blend
per_game = 0.5 × ros_per_game
        + 0.5 × cur_per_game

score = per_game × plays_today × mult
AB / 3.7 estimates games played (a typical hitter gets ~3.7 AB per game). The 50/50 blend lets recent form (hot/cold) influence today's decision instead of trusting projections alone. Players with no current-season ABs contribute 0 from the cur side — the blend naturally weights by sample size.
PITCHERS
if SP:
  ros_per_start = ros_sgp / (GS_proj || IP_proj/6)
  cur_per_start = cur_sgp / (IP_actual / 6)
  per_unit = 0.5×ros + 0.5×cur
  plays = (probable today)

if RP:
  ros_per_app = ros_sgp / G_proj
  cur_per_app = cur_sgp / IP_actual
  per_unit = (0.5×ros + 0.5×cur) × 0.4
  plays = team_plays_today

score = per_unit × plays × mult
Same 50/50 blend on the pitcher side. SPs only contribute on probable-today days (probables data tells us this exactly). RPs are downweighted to 40% of per-game value since they only appear in ~40% of their team's games. Pitchers with zero current-season IP contribute 0 from the cur side.

Why blend ROS + current? ROS projections regress hard toward league mean and lag real-world breakouts/slumps. Current-season rate captures recent form but is noisy on small samples. The 50/50 blend gets both: stability from ROS, responsiveness from current. A hot streak still gets noticed; a tiny rookie sample still gets ROS-level conservatism.

Matchup adjustment (the multiplier)Quartile-based, season-long, stable

Once the per-game/per-start raw score is computed, it's scaled by a multiplier based on the quality of who they're facing. Two separate quartile systems — one for hitters facing pitchers, one for pitchers facing lineups.

Hitters: opp SP quality buckets

Every pitcher's current-season ERA is sorted across the league and split into quartiles — what the SP is actually doing right now, not what FantasyPros projects forward. No IP minimum, so even rookies in their first start get bucketed. Lower ERA = better pitcher = tougher matchup for the hitter.

opp_sp_ERA ≤ Q1 (25th) → Tough → × 0.91  (−9%)
opp_sp_ERA ≤ Q2 (50th) → Above-avg → × 0.97  (−3%)
opp_sp_ERA ≤ Q3 (75th) → Avg → × 1.03  (+3%)
opp_sp_ERA > Q3 → Easy → × 1.09  (+9%)

Why ±9% instead of the ±15% the quartile labels suggest? Bullpen discount. Modern starters average ~5.5 IP, so only ~60% of a hitter's PAs face the listed SP. The other 40% face a generic bullpen, where matchup is essentially neutral. Final swing: 1 + 0.6 × (raw_mult − 1).

Why ERA, not ROS-SGP? Current ERA reflects how the SP is performing now. ROS projections regress hard toward league mean for unproven arms, so a rookie throwing 2.50 ERA across 3 starts gets projected as below-replacement going forward — making them look like an "Easy" matchup even though hitters are actually getting carved up. Trusting current production solves that.

Pitchers: opp lineup strength buckets

For each MLB team, the top 9 hitters' ROS-SGP-per-game is summed. All 30 teams sorted, split into quartiles. Today's SP faces the opposing team's lineup tier.

lineup_strength ≥ Q3 → Tough → × 0.85  (−15%)
lineup_strength ≥ Q2 → Above-avg → × 0.95  (−5%)
lineup_strength ≥ Q1 → Avg → × 1.05  (+5%)
lineup_strength < Q1 → Easy → × 1.15  (+15%)

Pitchers get the full ±15% — no bullpen discount because they face the entire lineup directly. RPs are not adjusted — their PA samples per appearance are too small for matchup quality to matter consistently.

Why season-long quartiles instead of today's slate? Today's 12-15 probables are too small a sample to define stable cutoffs — the line between "ace" and "above-avg" would shift meaningfully day-to-day. Season-long quartiles (computed from your existing FantasyPros pitcher data) give a stable answer to "is this guy a top-25% starter?" that doesn't drift with the slate.
Slot assignment algorithmGreedy, scarcity-ordered

After every player has a today-score, slots are filled in scarcity order — single-position slots first, flex slots last. At each step, the highest-scoring unassigned eligible player wins the slot.

for slot in [C, 1B, 2B, 3B, SS, CI, MI,
        OF (×5 flex), UT]:
  eligible = [p for p in roster
            if p not assigned
            and p.pos ∩ slot.eligible
            and p.plays_today]
  eligible.sort(by score desc)
  slot.picked = eligible[0..slot.count]

Why scarcity order? If you fill OF first and a player is eligible at both OF and 2B, they might steal an OF slot from someone less flexible — leaving 2B unfilled when a competent 2B was available. Going single-position first (where flexibility is lowest) makes sure rare-eligibility players land in the rare slots.

Off-day skip: A player whose MLB team isn't on today's schedule is excluded from the "eligible" pool entirely — they don't displace someone who is playing. They still appear in the ranked list (dimmed) so you can see the full bench.

Pitcher slots9 flexible P slots

The league's 9 P slots accept any pitcher type (SP/RP/P), so the optimizer ignores per-slot eligibility for pitchers entirely. It just sorts every pitcher on the roster by today-score and takes the top 9 with score > 0. Players with score = 0 (off day or SP not probable today) drop to the bench.

In practice this means probable SPs almost always win slots over RPs, and on light slate days (4 probable SPs) the 5 leftover slots fill with the highest-scoring RPs whose teams play.

Probables data — pipelinestatsapi.mlb.com → data/probables.json

The matchup multiplier needs to know who's pitching today. fetch_fantrax.py appends a block that hits MLB's free Stats API for the next 7 days of games with probable pitchers hydrated.

GET https://statsapi.mlb.com/api/v1/schedule
    ?sportId=1&startDate={today}&endDate={today+7}
    &hydrate=probablePitcher,team

Output: data/probables.json

{
  "fetched": "2026-05-08T18:33:00Z",
  "today": "2026-05-08",
  "games": [{
    "home": "PIT", "away": "CHC",
    "homeSP": { "id":..., "name": "Mike Burrows" },
    "awaySP": { "id":..., "name": "Shota Imanaga" }
  }, ...],
  "schedule": { "PIT":["2026-05-08",...], "CHC":[...], ... }
}

Failure mode: if the MLB API call fails, the script writes an empty payload with an error field rather than blowing up the whole pipeline. The Lineup Organizer's status pill reflects this: green "Matchup data on" / amber "Matchup data error" / amber "Matchup data missing".

Cross-source matchingThree independent ID systems

The Lineup Organizer joins three disjoint data sources. Getting them to agree took a few non-obvious bridges:

Fantrax roster — gives us _rosRosterLookup[normRos(name)] with team membership, and _rosCurrentStats[key].mlb with the player's MLB short code (e.g. "NYY").
FantasyPros ROS projections — _rosHitData / _rosPitData, keyed by _normPlayerKey(_displayName). Has G/AB for hitters, GS/IP for pitchers.
MLB Stats API probables — probables.json, with team abbreviations and SP fullName. Indexed by team abbreviation.
Bridge 1: name normalization

Names disagree across sources due to: accented characters (Jesús vs Jesus), suffixes (Jr/Sr/II/III), and inconsistent punctuation. _normPlayerKey() handles all three:

// 1. NFD-decompose to split é → e + combining acute
// Then strip combining marks (U+0300–U+036F)
raw = name.normalize('NFD').replace(/[\u0300-\u036f]/g, '')

// 2. Lowercase, drop non-letters, collapse whitespace
k = raw.toLowerCase().replace(/[^a-z ]/g, '')
     .replace(/\s+/g, ' ').trim()

// 3. Strip suffix tokens
k = k.replace(/\s+(jr|sr|ii|iii|iv)$/, '').trim()
Bridge 2: team-code aliasing

Fantrax and MLB Stats API use the same 3-letter code for ~25 of 30 teams, but diverge on a few. _LO_TEAM_ALIASES maps every known variant to one canonical form:

'TBR' → 'TB',   'CHW' → 'CWS',
'AZ' → 'ARI', 'WAS' → 'WSH',
'KCR' → 'KC',   'SDP' → 'SD',
'SFG' → 'SF',   'CUB' → 'CHC'

Both sides canonicalize before any lookup. New mismatches can be added to the table without other code changes.

Bridge 3: current-season ERA attached to FP records

The matchup tier comes from the SP's current-season ERA — not their FP ROS projection. ERA lives in _rosCurrentStats, but lookups go through FP records (which is where the SP-by-name index is built). So at index time, each FP record gets a copy of its current-season ERA attached:

// Index every SP, attach current-season ERA
cs = _rosCurrentStats[k]
p._currERA = (cs.IP > 0) ? cs.ERA : null
spByName[k] = p

// Quartiles from current ERA, no IP filter
allERAs = pitchers.filter(_currERA != null).map(_currERA)
quartiles = percentiles(sorted(allERAs))

Pitchers with no current-season IP (haven't appeared yet) fall through to a neutral "Avg" label rather than skewing the cutoffs.

Card anatomyWhat every visual element means

Each player card packs a lot of state into ~220px. Reading from top to bottom:

Name + score — left, right. Score color: dark green ≥ 0.50, medium green ≥ 0.25, default text otherwise. Below the score is the matchup multiplier (e.g. ×1.09) color-coded green for boost, amber for penalty. Hidden when matchup is exactly ×1.00.
Team line — e.g. "NYY vs TEX" (home) or "ATL @ LAD" (away). For off-day cards: just the team plus "off day."
Opp-pitcher strip (hitters only) — small avatar with SP initials, "vs Last Name" with handedness pill, current-season ERA / WHIP / K/9, then the matchup-tier pill (Tough/Above-avg/Avg/Easy). Full strip is hidden if the team is off, the SP isn't announced yet, or it's a pitcher card.
Tier pill (pitchers, fallback for hitters) — same color scheme: Tough = amber, Easy = green, Avg = neutral. RP cards always say "RP" (no adjustment).
Status tag — bottom of card. Green "In lineup" if optimizer picked them. Amber "Toss-up" if the next-best option is within 0.05 SGP. Italic gray "Off day" or "Not probable today" if they can't contribute.
OF (×5) flex row — OF appears as a single horizontally-scrolling row, ranked left to right. The 5 leftmost cards are the "In lineup" picks (labeled OF1-OF5). A thin vertical divider marks where the lineup ends. Cards past the divider are bench candidates; any card within 0.05 SGP of the cutoff line on either side is dashed-bordered as a "Toss-up." The slot label box shows "5 slots / N eligible" with an amber tint when there's a surplus.
Diagnostic helperDevTools console

If matchup labels look wrong (widespread "Matchup TBD", scores not changing, or a team's hitters all show no opp SP), open DevTools → Console on the Lineup Organizer page and run:

_loDiagnostics()

It prints, in order:

  • The MLB codes Fantrax uses for your roster
  • The MLB codes from probables.schedule
  • Any team-code mismatches between the two (after alias normalization)
  • How many of today's probable SPs resolve to FantasyPros pitcher data, with a list of any that don't

If everything resolves but cards still misbehave, the issue is downstream of resolution (e.g. quartile cutoffs not computing, or cards rendering before context builds).

Slot config (this league)14 hitting + 9 pitching

Defined in _LO_HIT_SLOTS and _LO_PIT_SLOTS at the top of the Lineup Organizer code. Order matters — this is the scarcity-fill order.

Hitting (14 slots)
C, 1B, 2B, 3B, SS — single position
CI — 1B or 3B
MI — 2B or SS
OF × 5 — any OF
UT — any hitter
Pitching (9 slots)
P × 9 — any pitcher (SP or RP)

Fully flexible — the optimizer just picks the 9 highest-scoring active pitchers each day. No SP/RP slot ratio enforced.
